---
title: "Methods (generated section)"
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
# Anchor on the git root: this report is its own Quarto project.
source(file.path(rprojroot::find_root(rprojroot::is_git_root), "R", "01_setup.R"))
clean <- get_clean_consumer_data()

n_total <- nrow(clean)
n_cond  <- nlevels(clean$condition)

# Keep the Overleaf-synced folder self-contained: copy the one shared
# bibliography into renders/manuscript/generated/ so main.tex resolves it both
# locally and on Overleaf (which only syncs the renders/manuscript/ folder).
gen_dir <- project_path("renders", "manuscript", "generated")
dir.create(gen_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
file.copy(project_path("references", "references.bib"),
          file.path(gen_dir, "references.bib"), overwrite = TRUE)
```

```{=latex}
% GENERATED FROM reports/manuscript/methods.qmd — DO NOT EDIT BY HAND.
% Edit the prose in the .qmd, then: quarto render reports/manuscript
```

\section{Methods}

We analysed a simulated consumer decision-making dataset of `r n_total`
participants, each randomly assigned to one of `r n_cond` message-framing
conditions (a plain control plus scarcity, sustainability, social-proof, and
combined frames). After viewing the framed product, participants reported
multi-item measures of price sensitivity, sustainability concern, brand trust,
and perceived value, followed by their purchase intention and a hypothetical
buy decision.

Multi-item scales were averaged into composite scores after reverse-scoring the
relevant brand-trust item, and a value of $-99$ was treated as missing. Data
were cleaned with scripted, version-controlled steps; the full codebook and
cleaning code live in the project repository. All models were estimated in R
with ordinary least squares (\texttt{lm}). Every figure and table in this paper
is generated directly from the analysis code, so the reported numbers cannot
drift from the analysis.
